## QUASI-NEWTON POLICY GRADIENT ALGORITHMS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Policy gradient algorithms have been widely applied to reinforcement learning (RL) problems in recent years. Regularization with various entropy functions is often used to encourage exploration and improve stability. In this paper, we propose a quasi-Newton method for the policy gradient algorithm with entropy regularization. In the case of Shannon entropy, the resulting algorithm reproduces the natural policy gradient (NPG) algorithm. For other entropy functions, this method results in brand new policy gradient algorithms. We provide a simple proof that all these algorithms enjoy the Newton-type quadratic convergence near the optimal policy. Using synthetic and industrial-scale examples, we demonstrate that the proposed quasi-Newton method typically converges in single-digit iterations, often orders of magnitude faster than other state-of-the-art algorithms.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Throughout the paper, the state space _S_ and the action space _A_ are assumed to be finite. A policy _π_ is a randomized rule of action-selection where _πs[a]_[denotes the probability of choosing action] _[ a]_[ at state] _[ s]_[.][For a given policy] _[ π]_[, the value function] _vπ_ defined as

This problem can be solved by, for example, the policy gradient (PG) method. However, the vanilla PG method converges quite slowly. In Agarwal et al. PG method with entropy regularization and some of its variants, the convergence rate can be improved to _O_ ( _e[−][cT]_ ), i.e., linear convergence (Mei et al., 2020), which can still be slow since _c_ is in general close to 0. It is also demonstrated in numerical examples that these algorithms with linear convergence rate can suffer from slow convergence. For example, in the example in Zhan et al. (2021), thousands of iterations are needed for the algorithm to converge, even though the model is relatively small and sparse. Therefore, there is a clear need for designing new methods with faster convergence and one idea is to take the geometry of the problem into consideration. The Newton method, for example, preconditions the gradient with the Hessian matrix and obtains second-order local convergence. Since the exact Hessian matrix is usually too computationally expensive to obtain, the quasi-Newton type methods, which use structurally simpler approximations of the Hessian instead, are more widely used in generic optimization problems, and are known to enjoy superlinear convergence (Rodomanov & Nesterov, 2021a;b).

## 1.1 CONTRIBUTIONS

In this paper, we investigate the quasi-Newton approach for solving (5). The main contributions of this paper are the following.

- First, we present a unified quasi-Newton type method for the policy optimization problem. The main observation is to decompose the Hessian as a sum of a diagonal part and a remainder, where at the optimal solution the remainder part vanishes so that the Hessian becomes diagonal. This inspires us to use only the diagonal part in the quasi-Newton method. As a result, the proposed method not only leverage the second order information but also enjoys low computational cost due to the diagonal structure of the preconditioner used. When the negative Shannon entropy is used, this method reproduces the natural policy gradient (NPG) algorithm. For other entropic regularizations, this method results in brand new policy gradient algorithms.

- Second, we analyze the convergence property of the proposed quasi-Newton algorithms and demonstrate local quadratic convergence both theoretically and numerically. By leveraging the quasi-Newton framework (Dennis & Mor´e, 1974), we provide a simple and straightforward proof for quadratic convergence near the optimal policy. In the numerical tests, we verify that the proposed method leads to fast quadratic convergence even under small regularization and large discount rate (close to 1). Even for industrial-size problems with hundreds of thousands states, the quasi-Newton method converges in single-digit iterations and within a few minutes on a regular laptop.

## 1.2 BACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK

A major workhorse behind the recent success in reinforcement learning (RL) is the large family of policy gradient (PG) methods (Williams, 1992; Sutton et al., 1999), for example, the natural policy gradient (NPG) method (Kakade, 2001), the actor-critic method (Konda & Tsitsiklis, 2000), the asynchronous advantage actor-critic (A3C) method (Mnih et al., 2016), the deterministic policy gradient (DPG) method (Silver et al., 2014), the trust region policy optimization (TRPO) (Schulman et al., 2015a), the generalized advantage estimation (GAE) (Schulman et al., 2015b), and proximal policy optimization (PPO) (Schulman et al., 2017), to mention but a few. The NPG method is known to be drastically faster than the original PG method, intuitively because in the NPG method the policy gradient is preconditioned by the Fisher information (an approximation of the Hessian of the KL-divergence) matrix and fits the problem geometry better. This idea is extended in TRPO and PPO where the problem geometry is taken into consideration via trust region constraints (in terms of KL-divergence) and a clipping function of the relative ratio of policies in the objective function, respectively. These implicit ways (in the sense that they do not adjust the gradient by an explicit preconditioner) of adjusting the policy gradient is in essence similar to the mirror descent (MD) method (Nemirovskij & Yudin, 1983) in generic optimization problems.

This similarity in addressing the inherent geometry of the problem is noticed by a line of recent work including Neu et al. (2017); Geist et al. (2019); Shani et al. (2020); Tomar et al. (2020); Lan (2021), and the analysis techniques in MD methods have been adapted to the PG setting. The connection was first built explicitly in Neu et al. (2017). The authors consider a linear program formulation where the objective function is the average reward and the domain is the set of stationary state-action

distributions, in which case the TRPO method can be viewed as an approximate mirror descent method and the A3C method as an MD method for the dual-averaging (Nesterov, 2009) objective. As a complement, Geist et al. (2019) considers an actor-critic type method where the policy is updated via either a regularized greedy step or an MD step and the value function is updated by a regularized Bellman operator, which also includes TRPO as a special case, and error propagation analysis is provided. In Shani et al. (2020), an adaptive scaling that naturally arises in the policy gradient is applied to the proximity term of the MD formulation, and sublinear convergence result is proved with a properly decreasing learning rate. In Tomar et al. (2020), the application to the nontabular setting is enabled by parameterizing the policy and applying MD to the policy parameters, and the corresponding sublinear convergence result is presented.

Regularization, a strategy that considers the modified objective function with an additional penalty term on the policy, is another crucial component in the development of PG type methods. Intuitively, regularization is able to encourage exploration in the policy iteration process and thus avoid local minima. It is also suggested (Ahmed et al., 2019) that regularization makes the optimization landscape smoother and thus enables possibly faster convergence. Linear convergence result is then established for regularized PG and NPG methods (Agarwal et al., 2020; Mei et al., 2020; Cen et al., 2020). In these relatively earlier works (Agarwal et al., 2020; Mei et al., 2020; Cen et al., 2020), the regularization usually takes the form of (negative) entropy or relative entropy. In the more recent work Lan (2021) and Zhan et al. (2021) that follow the MD type methods, the regularization is extended to general convex functions with the resulting Bregman divergences different from the KL-divergence and linear convergence is guaranteed as well.

However, most of these algorithms are of either sublinear or linear convergence except the entropy regularized NPG with full step length (which is a special case of the quasi-Newton method we propose), and even the linear convergence rate _O_ ( _e[−][cT]_ ) can be slow since _c_ can be close to zero. This motivates us to invent the quasi-Newton policy gradient method to be introduced in Section 2.

## 4 NUMERICAL EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 EXPERIMENT I

We first test the quasi-Newton methods derived in Section 2 on the model in Zhan et al. (2021). For the sake of completeness, we include the model description here. The MDP considered has a state space _S_ of size 200 and an action space _A_ of size 50. For each state _t_ and action _a_ , a subset _St[a]_[of] _[ S]_ is uniformly randomly chosen such that _|St[a][|]_[ = 20][, and] _[ P][ a] tt[′]_[= 1] _[/]_[20][ for any] _[ t][′][∈][S] t[a]_[.][The reward is] given by _rs[a]_[=] _[ U] s[ a][U][s]_[, where] _[ U] s[ a]_[and] _[ U][s]_[are independently uniformly chosen on][ [0] _[,]_[ 1]][.][The discount] rate _γ_ is set as 0 _._ 99 and the regularization coefficient _τ_ = 0 _._ 001.

In the numerical experiment, we implement Algorithm 1 with the KL divergence, the reverse KL divergence, the Hellinger divergence and the _α_ -divergence with _α_ = _−_ 3. We set the initial policy as the uniform policy, the convergence threshold as _ϵ_ = 10 _[−]_[12] and the learning rate _η_ as 1. Figure 1(a) demonstrates that, for these four tests, the quasi-Newton algorithm converges in 7, 7, 7, and 6 iterations, respectively. In comparison, we apply the policy mirror descent (PMD) and the general policy mirror descent (GPMD) method in Zhan et al. (2021) to the same MDP with the same stopping criterion. As also shown in Figure 1(a), many more iterations are needed for GPMD and PMD to reach the same precision: GPMD converges in 14822 iterations, and PMD does not reach the desired precision after 3 _×_ 10[5] iterations.

In order to verify the quadratic convergence proved in Section 3, we draw the plots of log _|_ log _∥π − π[∗] ∥F |_ in Figures 1(b), 1(c), 1(d) and 1(e), where _π[∗]_ is the final policy and the norm used is the Frobenius norm. A green reference line with slope log 2 through the origin is plotted for comparison. If the error converges exactly at a quadratic rate, the plot of log _|_ log _∥π − π[∗] ∥|_ shall be parallel to the reference line. The convergence curves approach the reference lines in the end (and are even steeper than the reference lines in the begining), demonstrating clearly a quadratic convergence for all regularizations used here.

## 4.2 EXPERIMENT II

Next, we apply the quasi-Newton methods derived in Section 2 to an MDP model constructed from the search logs of an online shopping store, with two different ranking strategies. Each issued query is represented as a state in the MDP. In response to a query, the search can be done by choosing one of the two ranking strategies (actions) to return a ranked list of products shown to the customer. Based on the shown products, the customer can refine or update the query, thus entering a new state. The reward at each state-action pair is a weighted sum of the clicks and purchases resulting from the action. Based on the data collected from two separate 5-week periods for both ranking strategies, we construct an MDP with 135k states, and a very sparse transition tensor _P_ with only 0 _._ 01% nonzero entries. The discount rate _γ_ is set as 0 _._ 99 and the regularization coefficient is _τ_ = 0 _._ 001.

## 5 DISCUSSION

In this paper, we present a fast quasi-Newton method for the policy gradient algorithm that is capable of solving large MDP problems with large ( _≈_ 1) discount rate and small ( _≈_ 0) regularizations. The proposed method includes the well-known natural policy gradient algorithm as a special case, and naturally extends to other regularizers such as the reverse KL divergence, the Hellinger divergence and the _α_ -divergence.

Theoretically, we show local quadratic convergence of the proposed quasi-Newton method with a relatively simple proof. This quadratic convergence is confirmed numerically on both medium and large sparse models. In contrast with mirror descent type methods (e.g. Zhan et al. (2021)) that take up to tens of thousands iterations even with manually tuned learning rate, the proposed quasiNewton algorithms typically converge in less than 10 iterations, despite the large discount rate (close to 1) and small regularization coefficient ( _τ ≈_ 0).

For future work, we plan to adapt the technique used here to other gradient based algorithms for solving the MDP problems. Other types of _f_ -divergence can also be included. Another direction is to consider continuous MDP problems by leveraging function approximation, effective spatial discretization, or model reduction.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper addresses an important and timely problem—accelerating policy gradient methods via second-order information—and the proposed idea of exploiting a diagonal decomposition of the Hessian that becomes exact at optimality is elegant and potentially impactful, particularly as it unifies NPG as a special case while generalizing to other entropy regularizers. The claimed contributions (unified quasi-Newton framework, quadratic local convergence proof, and strong empirical performance on industrial-scale problems) are well-motivated and the writing in the introduction is generally clear and well-structured, with appropriate situating within the PG/NPG/mirror-descent literature. However, several concerns arise even from the opening. First, the central theoretical claim—"Newton-type quadratic convergence"—is only local, and the paper should more carefully delineate the basin of attraction and conditions (e.g., strong convexity from regularization, dependence on τ and γ) rather than advertising quadratic convergence as a headline result; the tension between "even under small regularization" in the numerics and the regularization-dependence of the theory deserves scrutiny. Second, the novelty relative to NPG is somewhat understated: since the method reduces to NPG under Shannon entropy, the paper must clearly articulate what is genuinely new beyond a reinterpretation, and whether the new algorithms for other entropies yield practical advantages beyond those of NPG. Third, the motivating critique of existing methods leans heavily on worst-case rates (O(e^{-cT}) with c near zero), but modern PG analyses offer sharper bounds, and the empirical comparison should include strong baselines (NPG, TRPO, PPO, and recent accelerated/mirror-descent PG variants) in tabular settings where the method applies. Finally, the scope appears restricted to finite state/action MDPs with exact model knowledge—this is a significant limitation relative to the broader RL claims, and the applicability to sample-based or function-approximation settings (where most PG methods are deployed) should be addressed explicitly rather than obscured.

A careful reader could be misled about what Figure 2 represents. This is a real inconsistency between the text and the figure caption, though it doesn't affect the technical claims.

comments.title: PMD non-convergence claim lacks detail on learning rate / tuning
comments.quote: GPMD converges in 14822 iterations, and PMD does not reach the desired precision after 3 _×_ 10[5] iterations.
comments.explanation: The comparison with PMD/GPMD from Zhan et al. (2021) is a central empirical claim ('orders of magnitude faster'). However, the passage does not specify what learning rate β' was used for PMD/GPMD in these experiments, nor whether it was tuned. Since Zhan et al.'s method depends on a manually tuned learning rate (as the authors themselves note in Section 3: 'with a manually tuned learning rate β''), the fairness of the comparison depends on this choice. A reader cannot reproduce or evaluate this comparison from what is given here. This is a reproducibility/underspecification concern for a headline comparison.

comments.title: Bi-CGSTAB convergence tolerance not specified
comments.quote: When calculating _vπ_ by _vπ_ = ( _I − γPπ_ ) _[−]_[1] ( _rπ − τhπ_ ), we apply the iterative solver Bi-CGSTAB (Van der Vorst, 1992)
comments.explanation: The number of BiCGSTAB steps per quasi-Newton iteration is reported (Table 1), but the tolerance used to terminate BiCGSTAB is not stated. This matters because if the inner solve is not accurate enough, the claimed quadratic convergence of the outer iteration can be degraded; if it is too accurate, the inner cost is larger than necessary. A reader cannot reproduce the experiment without knowing this inner tolerance. Minor but relevant for reproducibility of an empirical result that is used to support a key contribution.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an interesting and potentially valuable direction—using entropy-induced second-order structure to accelerate tabular policy optimization—and the claimed unification of natural policy gradient with other entropy-regularized quasi-Newton updates is appealing. The introduction is generally well motivated and situates the work within policy-gradient and mirror-descent literature, but the presentation would benefit from sharper precision about the setting: the method appears to be model-based, finite-state/action, and likely requires exact transition/reward information and value solves, which substantially limits the relevance of comparisons to broader “state-of-the-art” deep RL algorithms unless this distinction is made explicit. The strongest concern is that several claims are very broad relative to the assumptions visible so far, especially “all these algorithms enjoy Newton-type quadratic convergence” and “industrial-scale” convergence in single-digit iterations; such statements require careful qualification regarding interior optimality, entropy smoothness/strong convexity, regularization strength, discount factor, step-size/globalization, and whether the diagonal Hessian approximation satisfies the Dennis–Moré conditions. The claim that the Hessian remainder vanishes at the optimum is central and should be explained early and rigorously, since it is not obvious for general entropy functions or constrained simplex policies. Clarity is also weakened by notation overload and formatting issues in the excerpt, particularly around Bellman equations and entropy definitions, and the paper should more clearly distinguish regularized rewards/value functions from unregularized ones. Overall, the paper seems promising and potentially impactful within exact tabular/model-based RL, but its quality will depend heavily on whether the theoretical assumptions are stated transparently, the quadratic-convergence proof is genuinely valid beyond Shannon entropy/NPG, and the empirical comparisons are fair in computational cost and problem setting.

comments.title: Quadratic convergence claims overstate the conditional theorem and empirical evidence
comments.quote: We provide a simple proof that all these algorithms enjoy the Newton-type quadratic convergence near the optimal policy. / Assume that ... θ^(k) → θ*. Then π^(k) converges quadratically to π*. / This quadratic convergence is confirmed numerically...
comments.explanation: The formal quadratic-convergence result is conditional: Theorem 5 is stated for the full-step case η = 1 and assumes θ^(k) → θ*, which is essentially convergence to the optimizer for the entropy maps considered. Thus it establishes an a posteriori quadratic rate once convergence is already known, not a full local-attraction theorem showing that sufficiently close initializations remain nearby and converge quadratically. Several surrounding statements are broader, referring to “all these algorithms” or “the proposed quasi-Newton method” without the full-step and convergence assumptions. The numerical evidence also uses the final policy of the same run as a proxy for π*, so it is better described as consistent with quadratic convergence rather than as a verification of the theorem.

comments.title: Newton step described as preconditioning by the Hessian rather than its inverse
comments.quote: The Newton method, for example, preconditions the gradient with the Hessian matrix and obtains second-order local convergence.
comments.explanation: The intended point is clear, but the Newton direction is obtained by multiplying by the inverse Hessian, or equivalently by solving a linear system with the Hessian. Saying the gradient is preconditioned with the Hessian itself is technically imprecise and could mislead readers, since multiplying by the Hessian generally does not produce a Newton direction.

comments.title: Quasi-Newton terminology and generic superlinear-convergence claims need qualification
comments.quote: the quasi-Newton type methods ... are known to enjoy superlinear convergence ... / In this paper, we use a more general definition of the term “quasi-Newton method.”
comments.explanation: Classical quasi-Newton methods such as BFGS can achieve superlinear convergence under additional assumptions, but quasi-Newton methods as a broad class do not automatically enjoy superlinear convergence merely because they use approximate Hessians. The paper also uses “quasi-Newton” more broadly than the standard secant-update meaning: the proposed approximation is analytically derived from Hessian structure and is closer to a structured or inexact Newton preconditioner. This is not necessarily wrong, but the text should clarify that the convergence proof relies on the specific approximation property established here, not on generic quasi-Newton theory.

comments.title: Constrained gradient is described as an unconstrained Euclidean gradient
comments.quote: The gradient of E(π) in R^[|S||A|] is ... where c_s is the Lagrange multiplier associated with the constraint ∑_a π_s[a] = 1.
comments.explanation: The expression with state-dependent constants c_s is appropriate for constrained first-order conditions, tangent-space directional derivatives, or a Lagrangian gradient. However, it is not literally the unique ambient Euclidean gradient in R^{|S||A|}. Since the subsequent Hessian decomposition and quasi-Newton preconditioning are stated in ambient coordinates, the paper should explicitly say that gradients are being considered modulo state-wise constants, or that the displayed object is the constrained/Lagrangian gradient.

comments.title: Regularization is said to avoid local minima without a clear guarantee
comments.quote: Intuitively, regularization is able to encourage exploration in the policy iteration process and thus avoid local minima.
comments.explanation: Entropy-style regularization does encourage exploration and interior policies, but the stronger claim that it avoids local minima is not generally guaranteed as stated. The paper is also formulated as a maximization problem, so “local minima” is imprecise unless referring to the negative objective. This is a mild overstatement in background prose rather than a flaw in the main derivation.

comments.title: Simplex constraint uses an apparently dimensionally inconsistent transpose
comments.quote: Define ... B the |S|-by-(|S|×|A|) matrix ... Assume that ... {π : B^⊤π = 1_|S|, π_s[a] > 0}
comments.explanation: With B defined as the block-summing matrix mapping a vectorized policy to per-state action-probability sums, the simplex constraint should be Bπ = 1_|S|. The expression B^⊤π is dimensionally invalid for the stated sizes. Later text appears to use Bπ correctly, so this is likely a transpose typo, but it occurs inside the formal assumptions of Theorem 5.

comments.title: Undiscounted terminal-state extension needs a stronger and clearer condition
comments.quote: All the policy update schemes can be extended to the case where γ = 1 and the MDP has a terminal state s_T and the Markov chain is irreducible. In that case we can remove s_T from S and replace γ with 1 in the update schemes.
comments.explanation: An absorbing terminal state makes the full Markov chain reducible unless the state space is trivial. More importantly, when γ = 1 the value equation after removing the terminal state is well-defined only under a transience/properness condition, such as spectral radius ρ(P̃^π) < 1 or almost-sure absorption with finite expected hitting time for the policies considered. Irreducibility alone does not guarantee this. The extension may be correct under standard stochastic-shortest-path assumptions, but those assumptions should be stated precisely.

comments.title: Lemma domain should be strict because σ is negative
comments.quote: Assume that σ < 0, then for any x_1, x_2, ..., x_k, there is a unique solution ... such that x + x_i ≥ 0
comments.explanation: Because σ < 0, terms of the form (x + x_i)^σ are not finite at x + x_i = 0. Any finite solution must therefore satisfy x + x_i > 0 for all i, not merely ≥ 0. This is a small domain-precision issue, but it matters for the stated interval and for implementations such as bisection near the lower endpoint.

comments.title: “Accelerated mirror descent” may overstate what the derivation shows
comments.quote: This suggests that (17) can be reinterpreted as an accelerated mirror descent method with adaptive learning rates β_s ≡ 1/(w^π(k))_s...
comments.explanation: The derivation supports an interpretation as a preconditioned or adaptively scaled mirror-descent step with state-wise learning rates. However, “accelerated mirror descent” usually refers to Nesterov-style acceleration mechanisms with momentum or estimate sequences and associated complexity guarantees. Unless such acceleration is intended and proved, “adaptive” or “preconditioned” mirror descent would be more precise.

comments.title: Baseline comparison lacks enough hyperparameter detail and is phrased too generally
comments.quote: we apply the policy mirror descent (PMD) and the general policy mirror descent (GPMD) method ... many more iterations are needed ... / In contrast with mirror descent type methods ... that take up to tens of thousands iterations...
comments.explanation: The experiments suggest that the proposed quasi-Newton updates require far fewer outer iterations on the tested instances. However, the PMD/GPMD comparison does not state the learning rates or other tuning choices, whether they were optimized for the instance, or whether the same regularizer and comparable accuracy measure were used. Since the reported gap is orders of magnitude, these details are important for reproducibility and fairness. The conclusion should also tie the performance claim to the tested experiments rather than implying a general property of mirror-descent methods as a class.

comments.title: Linear-solver reporting is insufficient to reproduce the BiCGSTAB work
comments.quote: we apply the iterative solver Bi-CGSTAB ... / For all the 4 regularizers used here, altogether only about 100 BiCGSTAB steps are needed...
comments.explanation: The paper reports BiCGSTAB iteration counts, but omits key inner-solver details: tolerance, residual norm, maximum iteration cap, initial guess, and whether a preconditioner was used. These choices can substantially affect both the number of BiCGSTAB steps and the accuracy of the value-function solves, which in turn can affect an inexact quasi-Newton method. In addition, the phrase “for all the 4 regularizers ... altogether only about 100” is ambiguous: the table appears to show roughly 100 BiCGSTAB steps per regularizer, not about 100 combined across all four runs.

comments.title: Construction of the industrial MDP is too underspecified to reproduce the experiment
comments.quote: Based on the data collected from two separate 5-week periods for both ranking strategies, we construct an MDP with 135k states, and a very sparse transition tensor P with only 0.01% nonzero entries.
comments.explanation: The application setup is understandable at a high level, but the MDP-estimation procedure is not described in enough detail to reproduce or assess the experiment. The paper does not specify how queries are canonicalized into 135k states, how transitions are estimated or smoothed, how unseen state-action pairs are handled, how the two data periods are combined, whether terminal or absorbing states are used, or how click and purchase rewards are weighted. These choices can materially affect the transition matrix, rewards, sparsity, and convergence behavior.

comments.title: Figure 2 caption appears to describe the wrong experiment
comments.quote: Figure 2: Figures for the synthetic medium scale MDP.
comments.explanation: Figure 1 is the synthetic medium-scale experiment, whereas Figure 2 appears in the industrial online-shopping search-log experiment and is described in the surrounding text as reporting the 135k-state MDP results. The caption could confuse readers about whether Figure 2 is synthetic or industrial-scale.

comments.title: NPG special-case claim is broader than what is established
comments.quote: The proposed method includes the well-known natural policy gradient algorithm as a special case...
comments.explanation: The paper appears to recover a specific entropy-regularized tabular natural policy gradient update, which is a useful connection. But “the well-known natural policy gradient algorithm” is broader, often referring to Fisher-preconditioned policy-gradient methods for unregularized or parameterized policies. The claim would be more precise if qualified as the entropy-regularized tabular NPG update or the particular NPG variant considered in the cited work.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an interesting and potentially valuable direction—using entropy-induced second-order structure to accelerate tabular policy optimization—and the claimed unification of natural policy gradient with other entropy-regularized quasi-Newton updates is appealing. The introduction is generally well motivated and situates the work within policy-gradient and mirror-descent literature, but the presentation would benefit from sharper precision about the setting: the method appears to be model-based, finite-state/action, and likely requires exact transition/reward information and value solves, which substantially limits the relevance of comparisons to broader “state-of-the-art” deep RL algorithms unless this distinction is made explicit. The strongest concern is that several claims are very broad relative to the assumptions visible so far, especially “all these algorithms enjoy Newton-type quadratic convergence” and “industrial-scale” convergence in single-digit iterations; such statements require careful qualification regarding interior optimality, entropy smoothness/strong convexity, regularization strength, discount factor, step-size/globalization, and whether the diagonal Hessian approximation satisfies the Dennis–Moré conditions. The claim that the Hessian remainder vanishes at the optimum is central and should be explained early and rigorously, since it is not obvious for general entropy functions or constrained simplex policies. Clarity is also weakened by notation overload and formatting issues in the excerpt, particularly around Bellman equations and entropy definitions, and the paper should more clearly distinguish regularized rewards/value functions from unregularized ones. Overall, the paper seems promising and potentially impactful within exact tabular/model-based RL, but its quality will depend heavily on whether the theoretical assumptions are stated transparently, the quadratic-convergence proof is genuinely valid beyond Shannon entropy/NPG, and the empirical comparisons are fair in computational cost and problem setting.

comments.title: Quadratic convergence claim is stronger than the stated theorem appears to justify
comments.quote: We provide a simple proof that all these algorithms enjoy the Newton-type quadratic convergence near the optimal policy.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a standard local convergence statement: if the initialization is sufficiently close to the optimal policy, the proposed iterations with the specified step size converge quadratically. Looking ahead to the formal result, however, Theorem 5 is stated for \(\eta=1\) and assumes, among other things, that \(\theta^{(k)} \to \theta^*\). That is closer to a conditional quadratic-rate result once convergence to the optimum is already known, rather than a full local convergence theorem establishing a basin of attraction. The abstract and contribution text also say “all these algorithms,” while the algorithm allows learning rates \(0<\eta\le 1\), and quadratic convergence is only asserted later for the full-step case. The authors may intend “quadratic rate conditional on being in the local convergent regime,” which is plausible for a quasi-Newton analysis, but the present wording overstates what the formal theorem, as written, establishes.

comments.title: Newton step described as preconditioning by the Hessian rather than its inverse
comments.quote: The Newton method, for example, preconditions the gradient with the Hessian matrix and obtains second-order local convergence.
comments.explanation: The intended point is clear: Newton-type methods use curvature information and can achieve quadratic local convergence under standard regularity assumptions. However, in optimization the Newton direction is obtained by multiplying the gradient by the inverse Hessian, or equivalently by solving a linear system with the Hessian. Saying that the gradient is “preconditioned with the Hessian matrix” is technically imprecise and could be misleading because multiplying by the Hessian itself would generally not produce a Newton direction. This is a localized introductory imprecision, and the later quasi-Newton derivation appears to use an inverse/preconditioner in the expected way.

comments.title: Generic quasi-Newton methods are overstated as known to enjoy superlinear convergence
comments.quote: Since the exact Hessian matrix is usually too computationally expensive to obtain, the quasi-Newton type methods, which use structurally simpler approximations of the Hessian instead, are more widely used in generic optimization problems, and are known to enjoy superlinear convergence (Rodomanov & Nesterov, 2021a;b).
comments.explanation: The high-level motivation is reasonable: classical quasi-Newton methods such as BFGS can achieve superlinear local convergence. But the wording suggests that quasi-Newton methods as a class are generally known to enjoy superlinear convergence merely because they use approximate Hessians. In standard optimization theory, superlinear convergence requires additional assumptions, such as suitable secant updates, nonsingularity/regularity conditions, line-search or trust-region safeguards, and a Dennis–Moré-type condition; many Hessian approximations yield only linear convergence or may fail without safeguards. Since the paper later relies on a special diagonal approximation whose error vanishes at the optimizer, this broad statement should be qualified rather than presented as a generic property of quasi-Newton methods.

comments.title: Constrained gradient is described as an unconstrained Euclidean gradient
comments.quote:
The gradient of _E_ ( _π_ ) in R _[|][S][||][A][|]_ is

where _cs_ is the Lagrange multiplier associated with the constraint[] _a[π] s[a]_[= 1][.]
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: because feasible policy perturbations satisfy \(\sum_a \epsilon_s[a]=0\), adding a state-dependent constant to all action components does not change directional derivatives on the simplex, and the constants \(c_s\) are naturally used later to enforce normalization. However, as written this is not literally the gradient of \(E(\pi)\) in the ambient Euclidean space \(\mathbb{R}^{|S||A|}\). The Euclidean gradient is unique and should not contain arbitrary Lagrange multipliers; the expression with \(c_s\) is instead a gradient of a Lagrangian, a projected/tangent-space representation, or a representative of an equivalence class modulo state-wise constants. This distinction matters because the subsequent Hessian decomposition and quasi-Newton preconditioning are stated in the ambient space. The issue is fixable by explicitly saying that the formula is for the constrained first-order condition / Lagrangian gradient, or that gradients are considered modulo vectors constant over actions at each state.

comments.title: Regularization is said to avoid local minima without a clear guarantee
comments.quote: Intuitively, regularization is able to encourage exploration in the policy iteration process and thus avoid local minima.
comments.explanation: The exploration part is standard and consistent with entropy regularization. What remains problematic is the stronger phrase “avoid local minima.” In this paper the optimization problem is posed as a maximization, so “local minima” is already somewhat mismatched unless the authors are implicitly referring to minimizing the negative objective. More substantively, entropy or relative-entropy regularization can smooth the landscape and encourage interior policies, but it does not in general guarantee avoidance of all bad local optima/minima for arbitrary parameterizations or non-tabular settings. In the tabular discounted setting there are special structural results, but those are not what this sentence states. This is a mild overstatement in background prose rather than a flaw in the derivation.

comments.title: The paper broadens “quasi-Newton” beyond the standard meaning but then leans on standard terminology
comments.quote: In this paper, we use a more general definition of the term “quasi-Newton method”. Unlike classical quasi-Newton methods where the approximate Hessian matrix is constructed using firstorder information, the proposed quasi-Newton method uses second-order information, but the approximate Hessian matrix remains simple and its inverse is easy to obtain.
comments.explanation: The authors do acknowledge that their usage is broader than the classical one, which helps. Still, a careful reader may be left uncertain about what properties of quasi-Newton theory are intended to transfer. In standard numerical optimization, quasi-Newton methods typically build Hessian or inverse-Hessian approximations from secant information, i.e. gradients and iterates, and their convergence theory depends on secant-type approximation conditions. Here the approximation is analytically derived from the Hessian structure and is closer to a structured/inexact Newton preconditioner. This is not necessarily wrong, but when later claims invoke quasi-Newton analysis and Newton-type convergence, the terminology could mislead unless the authors clearly state that the convergence proof relies on a Dennis–Moré-type approximation condition rather than on a classical BFGS/DFP-style update mechanism.

comments.title: Simplex constraint uses an apparently dimensionally inconsistent transpose
comments.quote: where we denote by B the |S|-by-_ ( _|S|×|A|_ ) _matrix such that Bij_ = 1 _for |A|_ ( _i −_ 1)+1 _≤ j ≤|A|i and Bij_ = 0 _otherwise. Define_ ... _Assume that (1)][ ∇][f][is Lipschitz on a closed subset of] {π_ : _B[⊤] π_ = **1** _|S|, πs[a][>]_[ 0] _[}
comments.explanation: The intent is clear: B is the block-summing matrix that maps a vectorized policy to its per-state action-probability sums, so the simplex constraint should be Bπ = 1_{|S|}. This is also confirmed later in the mirror-descent discussion, where the text uses Bπ = Bπ^{(k)} = 1_{|S|}. With B defined as |S| by |S||A| and π vectorized over state-action pairs, B^⊤π is not dimensionally valid. This is likely just a transpose typo, but it appears inside the formal assumptions of Theorem 5 and could confuse the constrained-space statement.

comments.title: Undiscounted terminal-state extension needs a stronger/clearer condition than irreducibility
comments.quote: **Remark 4.** _All the policy update schemes can be extended to the case where γ_ = 1 _and the MDP has a terminal state sT and the Markov chain is irreducible. In that case we can remove sT from S and replace γ with_ 1 _in the update schemes.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended episodic construction: remove the absorbing terminal state and use the substochastic transition matrix on nonterminal states, so that I - \tilde P^π replaces I - γP^π. However, as stated, a Markov chain with an absorbing terminal state is not irreducible on the full state space unless the state space is trivial. More importantly, for γ = 1 the inverse/value equation is well-defined only under a transience/properness condition, e.g. spectral radius ρ(\tilde P^π) < 1 for the policies under consideration, or almost-sure absorption with finite expected hitting time. Irreducibility of the nonterminal subchain alone would not guarantee this if there is no leakage to the terminal state under some policy. The update may be correct under standard stochastic-shortest-path assumptions, but those assumptions need to be stated precisely.

comments.title: Quadratic convergence theorem assumes convergence to the optimizer
comments.explanation: The Dennis–Moré style argument can indeed establish a quadratic rate once the iterates are already converging to a nondegenerate solution and the Hessian approximation error is first order. But assumption (2), θ^{(k)} → θ^*, is already essentially convergence to the optimizer, since θ = φ'(|A|π) is invertible on the interior for the entropies considered. Thus the theorem as written proves an a posteriori rate conditional on convergence, not a full local convergence result of the usual form: if the initial policy is sufficiently close to π^*, then the iterates are well-defined, remain nearby, and converge quadratically. This is a weaker statement than the surrounding prose, which says the section “stud[ies] the quadratic convergence ... near” the optimum. The result may be standardly fixable, but the current theorem should either be framed as conditional quadratic rate or supplemented with a local-attraction argument.

comments.title: Lemma domain should be strict because σ is negative
comments.quote:
**Lemma 3.** _Assume that σ <_ 0 _, then for any x_ 1 _, x_ 2 _, . . . , xk, there is a unique solution to the equation:_

_such that x_ + _xi ≥_ 0 _, i_ = 1 _,_ 2 _, . . . , k.
comments.explanation: The lemma is being used in exactly the right way: with σ = 2/(α−1) < 0, solving a one-dimensional normalization equation determines the Lagrange multiplier c_s. However, because σ < 0, terms of the form (x+x_i)^σ are not finite at x+x_i = 0. Any actual solution to a finite equation like Σ_i (x+x_i)^σ = 1 must therefore satisfy x+x_i > 0 for every i, not merely ≥ 0. This is a small domain-precision issue, but it matters for the stated interval and for bisection implementation, where the lower endpoint should be treated as an open boundary or approached from above.

comments.title: Reverse-KL case should be described as a limit of the α-divergence formula
comments.quote: When α_ = _−_ 1 _(the reverse-KL divergence), the corresponding update scheme is πs[a][←]_  1 _π−s[a] η[−][η][|][A][|]_[(] _[r] s[a][−]_[[(] _[I][−][γP][ a]_[)] _[v][π]_[]] _[s]_[)] _[/τ]_[+] _[ c][s]_  _−_ 1 _, which can be obtained by inserting α_ = _−_ 1 _._
comments.explanation: The intended update is plausible: the α → −1 limit gives the reverse-KL regularizer and leads to an inverse-form update. But the α-divergence generating function used just above has a factor 1/(1−α^2), so the divergence itself is singular at α = −1 and reverse-KL is obtained only after taking the limit, up to additive/scaling constants. If equation (13) has already been algebraically simplified so that φ' and φ'' have well-defined limits, substituting α = −1 into that simplified expression is harmless. Still, the wording “inserting α = −1” is slightly misleading in light of the preceding definition; “taking the limit α → −1” would be mathematically safer.

comments.title: “Accelerated mirror descent” may overstate what the derivation shows
comments.quote: This suggests that (17) can be reinterpreted as an _accelerated_ mirror descent method with _adaptive_ learning rates _βs ≡_ 1 _/_ ( _wπ_ ( _k_ )) _s_ that depend on the state _s_ and the current iterate _π_[(] _[k]_[)] .
comments.explanation: The connection to mirror descent is useful and the adaptive state-wise steps β_s = 1/w_s^π do explain why the update differs from a standard fixed-stepsize mirror descent scheme. My concern is only with the word “accelerated”: in optimization, accelerated mirror descent usually refers to a specific Nesterov-style acceleration mechanism with momentum/estimate sequences and associated complexity guarantees. Equation (20) instead shows a preconditioned or adaptively scaled mirror descent step. Unless the authors intend and prove acceleration in the standard sense, calling it “adaptive/preconditioned mirror descent” would avoid a possible overclaim.

comments.title: Baseline comparison is not reproducible or clearly fair without PMD/GPMD hyperparameters
comments.quote: In comparison, we apply the policy mirror descent (PMD) and the general policy mirror descent (GPMD) method in Zhan et al. (2021) to the same MDP with the same stopping criterion. As also shown in Figure 1(a), many more iterations are needed for GPMD and PMD to reach the same precision: GPMD converges in 14822 iterations, and PMD does not reach the desired precision after 3 _×_ 10[5] iterations.
comments.explanation: The qualitative point is plausible: the proposed quasi-Newton update has an adaptive second-order scaling, while PMD/GPMD typically depend strongly on a chosen learning rate or regularization parameter. I checked the surrounding context, and Section 3 even emphasizes that the Zhan et al. method uses a manually tuned learning rate. However, this experiment does not state what learning rates or other tuning choices were used for PMD and GPMD, whether they were optimized for this instance, or whether the comparison used the same regularizer. Because the numerical claim is an orders-of-magnitude speed comparison, these missing details materially affect reproducibility and fairness. Also, the “same precision” appears to refer to the relative policy-change stopping rule rather than an objective gap or optimality residual, which is not necessarily a method-independent measure of accuracy.

comments.title: Linear-solver stopping criteria are missing despite reported BiCGSTAB iteration counts
comments.quote: When calculating _vπ_ by _vπ_ = ( _I − γPπ_ ) _[−]_[1] ( _rπ − τhπ_ ), we apply the iterative solver Bi-CGSTAB (Van der Vorst, 1992), a widely used numerical method with high efficiency and robustness for solving large sparse nonsymmetric systems of linear equations (Saad, 2003; de Pillis, 1998), in order to leverage the sparsity of the transition tensor.
comments.explanation: Using BiCGSTAB for the large sparse linear systems is a reasonable implementation choice, and the subsequent table of linear-solver steps is useful. What remains unclear is the linear-solver tolerance, residual norm, maximum iteration cap, initial guess, and whether any preconditioner was used. These choices can substantially change both the number of BiCGSTAB steps and the accuracy of the computed value function, and in an inexact Newton/quasi-Newton method they can also affect the observed convergence behavior. Since the paper reports very small outer tolerance, _ϵ = 10^{-12}_, and emphasizes that only about 100 BiCGSTAB steps are needed, the omitted inner-solve criteria are important for reproducing and interpreting the numerical results.

comments.title: Construction of the industrial MDP is too underspecified to reproduce the experiment
comments.quote: Based on the data collected from two separate 5-week periods for both ranking strategies, we construct an MDP with 135k states, and a very sparse transition tensor _P_ with only 0 _._ 01% nonzero entries. The discount rate _γ_ is set as 0 _._ 99 and the regularization coefficient is _τ_ = 0 _._ 001.
comments.explanation: The high-level description makes the application understandable: queries are states, ranking strategies are actions, query refinements induce transitions, and clicks/purchases define rewards. However, the actual MDP-estimation procedure is not described in enough detail for a reader to reproduce or assess the experiment. For example, the passage does not specify how queries are canonicalized into 135k states, how transitions are estimated and smoothed, how unseen state-action pairs are handled, how the two 5-week periods are combined, whether terminal or absorbing states are used, or what weights are used in the click/purchase reward. Some omission is understandable for proprietary industrial data, but these choices can materially affect the transition matrix, rewards, sparsity, and convergence results.

comments.title: Empirical quadratic-convergence claim relies on final iterate as a proxy for the optimizer
comments.quote: In order to verify the quadratic convergence proved in Section 3, we draw the plots of log _|_ log _∥π − π[∗] ∥F |_ in Figures 1(b), 1(c), 1(d) and 1(e), where _π[∗]_ is the final policy and the norm used is the Frobenius norm.
comments.explanation: The diagnostic itself is mathematically sensible: if the error satisfies roughly _e_{k+1} \approx C e_k^2_, then _\log |\log e_k|_ should grow asymptotically with slope _\log 2_ versus iteration. The concern is that the plotted _π^∗_ is not an independently computed optimizer but simply the final policy of the same run. With only 5–7 quasi-Newton iterations and a stopping rule based on relative policy change, this proxy can make the late-stage error plot look favorable without independently validating the true error to the optimizer. Thus the figures are good supporting evidence, but the wording “verify” and “demonstrating clearly” slightly overstates what this numerical check establishes.

comments.title: Ambiguous statement of total BiCGSTAB work across regularizers
comments.quote: For all the 4 regularizers used here, altogether only about 100 BiCGSTAB steps are needed in the whole training process, thanks to the fast convergence of the quasi-Newton method.
comments.explanation: The table immediately below helps infer the intended meaning: each individual regularizer uses about 83–110 total BiCGSTAB steps. Read literally, however, “For all the 4 regularizers used here, altogether only about 100” could mean about 100 steps combined across all four runs, which contradicts the table totals of 110, 109, 110, and 83, i.e. about 412 combined. This is likely just imprecise wording, but it could mislead readers about the computational cost.

comments.title: Figure 2 caption appears to describe the wrong experiment
comments.quote: Figure 2: Figures for the synthetic medium scale MDP.
comments.explanation: Figure 1 is indeed for the synthetic medium-scale MDP in Experiment I. Figure 2, however, appears in Experiment II and its panels are described in the surrounding text as results for the 135k-state online-shopping search-log MDP. This is not merely a stylistic caption issue: a reader could reasonably be confused about whether Figure 2 reports another synthetic test or the industrial-scale experiment. The surrounding section resolves the likely intent, so the issue is localized and easy to fix.

comments.title: NPG special-case claim is slightly broader than what is established
comments.quote: The proposed method includes the well-known natural policy gradient algorithm as a special case, and naturally extends to other regularizers such as the reverse KL divergence, the Hellinger divergence and the _α_ -divergence.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended connection: earlier Theorem 1 and Remark 1 show that, with the negative Shannon entropy/KL regularizer, the derived update matches the entropy-regularized natural policy gradient scheme used in Cen et al. (2020). That is a meaningful and useful observation. However, the wording here says the method includes “the well-known natural policy gradient algorithm” without the regularized/tabular/full-step qualifications. Standard NPG usually refers more broadly to Fisher-preconditioned policy-gradient methods, often for unregularized objectives and/or parameterized policies. The paper’s derivation appears to recover a specific entropy-regularized tabular NPG update, not the full class of NPG algorithms. This is fixable by saying “the entropy-regularized tabular NPG update” or similar.

comments.title: Quadratic-convergence statement omits important qualifications
comments.quote: Theoretically, we show local quadratic convergence of the proposed quasi-Newton method with a relatively simple proof. This quadratic convergence is confirmed numerically on both medium and large sparse models.
comments.explanation: The sentence correctly says “local,” which addresses one major qualification. But after checking the theorem in context, the formal result is for learning rate \(\eta=1\) and assumes, among other things, \(\theta^{(k)}\to\theta^\ast\) and Lipschitz conditions on an interior subset. Since Algorithm 1 allows a general learning rate \(\eta\), the phrase “the proposed quasi-Newton method” could be read as applying to all step sizes and all regimes. Also, numerical experiments can support or illustrate quadratic behavior, but “confirmed” may overstate what finite empirical diagnostics establish. A more precise formulation would mention the full-step/local setting and say the experiments are “consistent with” or “demonstrate empirically” quadratic convergence.

comments.title: Performance comparison is phrased more generally than the experiments justify
comments.quote: In contrast with mirror descent type methods (e.g. Zhan et al. (2021)) that take up to tens of thousands iterations even with manually tuned learning rate, the proposed quasiNewton algorithms typically converge in less than 10 iterations, despite the large discount rate (close to 1) and small regularization coefficient ( _τ ≈_ 0).
comments.explanation: The comparison is directionally supported by the experiments described earlier: on the tested synthetic and industrial-scale sparse MDPs, the quasi-Newton methods converge in single-digit iterations, while PMD/GPMD-type baselines require far more iterations. What remains problematic is the scope implied by “mirror descent type methods” and “typically.” As written, this sounds like a general empirical property of these method classes, whereas the evidence appears to come from a small number of model-based finite-MDP experiments with particular stopping criteria, regularization choices, and tuned baselines. The claim would be safer if tied explicitly to “our experiments” or “the tested instances.”

comments.title: “Small regularizations” is imprecise relative to the formal setup
comments.quote: large MDP problems with large ( _≈_ 1) discount rate and small ( _≈_ 0) regularizations.
comments.explanation: From the context, the intended meaning is clear: the experiments use discount factors close to one and small regularization coefficients, e.g. \(\tau=0.001\). The later sentence correctly says “small regularization coefficient.” But here “regularizations” could be read as the regularizer functions themselves being close to zero, rather than the scalar coefficient \(\tau\) being small. This is only a wording issue, but because the paper’s behavior depends sensitively on \(\tau\), it would be clearer to say “small regularization coefficients.”
